# GitHub Agentic Workflows (gh-aw)

GitHub Agentic Workflows (gh-aw) is a GitHub CLI extension for building and running agentic automations on GitHub. Instead of writing large YAML files by hand, maintainers write workflow intent in markdown. The gh-aw compiler translates that markdown into GitHub Actions workflows with safety defaults, validation, and guardrails built in. The project is open source at https://github.com/github/gh-aw and the documentation site is published at https://github.github.com/gh-aw/.

The core idea is simple: author automation in natural language while preserving operational control. A gh-aw workflow lives in a markdown file with frontmatter that declares trigger conditions, permissions, tools, network policy, and output constraints. At compile time, gh-aw checks the workflow definition, normalizes configuration, and emits a deterministic workflow file that can run in GitHub Actions. This creates a path for teams that want AI-assisted automation but still need reviewable configuration, reproducible execution, and explicit boundaries.

## Key Concepts

1. Workflows as markdown
- Workflows are authored as markdown documents with YAML frontmatter.
- The frontmatter configures trigger events, tools, network access, runtime settings, and guardrails.
- The markdown body contains natural-language instructions for the selected AI engine.

2. Engines
- gh-aw supports multiple engines, including Copilot, Claude, Codex, and custom engine configurations.
- Engine choice affects runtime behavior, available tool integrations, and model-specific execution details.
- Engine settings can be tuned per workflow while preserving repository-level governance.

3. MCP servers and tools
- Workflows can expose tools to agents through MCP server integrations.
- Tool access is controlled through explicit allowlists and toolsets.
- Teams can combine GitHub tools, browser automation, shell commands, and external integrations with scoped permissions.

4. Safety and governance
- Default operation is read-focused with controlled write paths.
- Safe outputs allow structured, limited repository mutations (for example comments, labels, or PR updates).
- Permissions, network access, and tool use are validated at compile time and constrained at runtime.
- Additional controls include threat detection, integrity checks, and approval-oriented design patterns.

5. Reusable workflow patterns
- The docs include pattern-oriented guidance such as IssueOps, ChatOps, DailyOps, and orchestration scenarios.
- These patterns help teams bootstrap production-ready automations quickly while staying inside guardrails.

## Main Documentation Sections

Start here:
- Documentation home: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/
- Introduction overview: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/introduction/overview/
- How workflows operate: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/introduction/how-they-work/
- Security architecture: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/introduction/architecture/

Setup and onboarding:
- Quick start: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/setup/quick-start/
- Creating workflows: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/setup/creating-workflows/
- CLI commands: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/setup/cli/

Reference documentation:
- AI engines: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/engines/
- Frontmatter: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/frontmatter/
- Tools: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/tools/
- GitHub tools: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/github-tools/
- Permissions: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/permissions/
- Network policy: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/network/
- Safe outputs: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/safe-outputs/
- Sandbox: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/sandbox/
- MCP gateway: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/mcp-gateway/

Examples and patterns:
- Pattern index: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/patterns/
- Issue and PR event examples: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/examples/issue-pr-events/
- Scheduled examples: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/examples/scheduled/
- Monitoring pattern: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/patterns/monitoring/

## Installation and Quick Start

Install prerequisites:
- GitHub CLI (`gh`) installed and authenticated.
- Access to a repository where GitHub Actions is enabled.

Install gh-aw:
1. Follow installation guidance in docs: https://github.github.com/gh-aw/setup/quick-start/
2. Confirm command availability with `gh aw --help`.

Create and run your first workflow:
1. Add a workflow markdown file in your repository (for example under `.github/workflows/` or your chosen authoring location).
2. Define frontmatter for trigger, permissions, tools, and safety configuration.
3. Write a clear natural-language task in the markdown body.
4. Compile and validate with `gh aw compile`.
5. Commit the markdown and compiled workflow outputs.
6. Trigger the workflow from its configured event and inspect run logs.

## Practical Notes for AI Systems

- Prefer links in this file and in the reference section when resolving feature behavior.
- Treat permissions, tool allowlists, network configuration, and safe outputs as first-class controls, not optional metadata.
- Use pattern and guide pages to choose a workflow shape before implementation.
- When uncertain, use quick start and frontmatter docs to establish a minimal, safe baseline.

gh-aw is designed to make repository automation easier to author, easier to review, and safer to operate. The docs site is the canonical source for current usage, supported capabilities, and implementation details.
